warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.foodCategory: All foods (including seasonings and condiments, snack foods)
rules.limit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions for permitted food additive use. Users must consult the GSFA Online Database and its Preamble for specific provisions.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database provides, in a searchable format, all the provisions for food additives that have been adopted by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

rules.foodCategory: All food and feed commodities (including seasonings and condiments, snack foods)
rules.limit: Maximum levels (MLs) must be legally permitted concentrations
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted in a commodity to protect human health. These levels are kept as low as possible based on scientific evidence.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.foodCategory: All food and feed commodities (including seasonings and condiments, snack foods)
rules.limit: Verification required via General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses permitted maximum levels or guideline levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants, detailed in CXS 193-1995.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXS 193-1995 General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed

rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) provides guidance for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.country: European Union (specifically Sweden, Finland, Norway, and other countries seeking equivalence)
rules.foodCategory: Certain live animals and products (e.g., poultry, meat, eggs, which can be ingredients for seasonings/snack foods)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Special guarantees for Salmonella control apply to trade from Sweden, Finland, and Norway. Other EU countries can obtain equivalent guarantees by demonstrating equivalent control programmes.
rules.evidenceQuote: Special guarantees as regard Salmonella When Sweden and Finland joined the EU special guarantees on Salmonella were provided as regards trade from other countries of certain live animals and products... The current guarantees are laid down in: Council Decision 95/410/EC... Decision 2004/235/EC... Decision 2003/644/EC... Regulation (EC) No 1688/2005... Other EU countries or region of EU countries can obtain special guarantees if it has a control programme recognised as equivalent...

rules.foodCategory: All food categories (including seasonings, snack foods)
rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: The database informs about approved food additives and their conditions of use, based on Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: These criteria define the acceptability of a product or a batch of food placed on the market.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.parameter: Laboratory competence for Listeria monocytogenes shelf-life studies
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: This document aims to harmonise the evaluation of laboratory competence for shelf-life studies (challenge tests and durability studies) related to Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods, ensuring compliance with Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: The EURL Lm Guidance Document to evaluate the competence of laboratories implementing challenge tests and durability studies related to Listeria monocytogenes in ready-to-eat foods aims to set up a harmonised approach to evaluate the competence of laboratories conducting shelf-life studies (challenge tests and durability studies), in order to comply with the food safety criteria defined in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.

rules.parameter: Listeria monocytogenes sampling in processing areas
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: These guidelines describe sampling procedures for food business operators to detect L. monocytogenes on surfaces of RTE food processing areas and equipment.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Guidelines on sampling the food processing areas and equipment describes sampling procedures to be performed by food business operators manufacturing RTE food which may pose a L. monocytogenes risk for public health in order to detect L. monocytogenes on the surfaces of RTE food processing areas and equipment.

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: This guidance document provides detailed information on conducting shelf-life studies (durability studies and challenge tests) for Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Technical guidance document on shelf-life studies for Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods provides specialised laboratories with detailed and practical information on how to conduct shelf-life studies (especially durability studies and challenge tests) for Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods.

rules.foodCategory: All food categories (including seasonings, snack foods)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: These criteria indicate the correct functioning of the production process.
rules.evidenceQuote: In addition, this Regulation lays down certain process hygiene criteria to indicate the correct functioning of the production process.

rules.foodCategory: Prepackaged food stuffs (seasoning, snack food, processed foods)

rules.notes: Specific labelling requirements for prepackaged food stuffs, including mandatory information, allergen declarations, and warnings, must be verified by consulting the full text of GSO 9:2022.

rules.notes: Specific microbiological limits for various foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified by consulting the full text of GSO 1016:2015.
rules.evidenceQuote: microbiological limits for some foodstuffs intended for human consumption and for some food ingredients used in food industry.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.parameter: Combined Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
rules.limit: Calculation required using SFA's Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to determine permitted levels
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Applies when two or more Class II chemical preservatives are used in the food.
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA and permitted for use
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: An application can be submitted to SFA to evaluate the use of an additive not currently permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Businesses must ensure additives are on the permitted list before importing or selling.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives and corresponding regulations
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Specific limits vary depending on the additive and the type of food product.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.parameter: Awareness and testing for common contaminants
rules.limit: Food businesses must be aware and conduct laboratory tests as needed
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Businesses can check with suppliers or send samples to SFA-recognized laboratories.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with these limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Applies if laboratory test results indicate contaminants exceed specified limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.foodCategory: general food (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.parameter: Specific contaminant limits (heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues, other incidental constituents)
rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's relevant documents
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Various limits are imposed on different types of contaminants.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: [...]

rules.foodCategory: Meat and food derived from meat (potentially relevant if seasoning/snacks contain meat ingredients)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Prohibition under Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)

rules.foodCategory: various food commodities (potentially seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks if they contain animal-derived ingredients)
rules.parameter: Veterinary drug residues (other than those prohibited)
rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's list of Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: MRLs for veterinary drug residues have been established for compliance by food businesses.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.foodCategory: prepacked foods (including seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.foodCategory: ready-to-eat snacks, seasoning powder, seasoning sauce (general food)
rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's relevant documents for ready-to-eat and non-ready-to-eat food
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Specific microbiological standards apply to ready-to-eat and non-ready-to-eat foods, with different effective dates for non-ready-to-eat food.
rules.evidenceQuote: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Specific exemption for ready-to-eat (RTE) food.
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.sourceTitle: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient
rules.notes: Specific use restrictions and labeling requirements for γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) produced by microbial fermentation as a food ingredient must be verified.
rules.evidenceQuote: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient

rules.parameter: Cephalopods and cephalopod products, Spiral shells and Spiral shell products, Seeds and seed products (excluding highly refined/purified sunflower seed oil), Kiwis and kiwi products
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: Food allergen labeling is recommended if these allergens are present as an ingredient or food additive.
rules.evidenceQuote: Besides the mandatory labeling allergens, food allergen labeling is also recommended for the products that contain any of the following allergens as an ingredient or food additive

rules.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, sulphite
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: Food allergen labeling is required if these allergens are present as an ingredient or food additive. For sulphite, the limit applies as residue sulphur dioxide.
rules.evidenceQuote: For prepackaged foods, food allergen labeling is required for the products that contain any of the following 11 allergens as an ingredient or food additive, regulated under Point 2 of the Regulation of Food Allergen Labeling: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, and sulphite as the residue sulphur dioxide for 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food.

rules.sourceTitle: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Foods
rules.notes: Specific limits for pesticide residues in foods must be verified in the full regulation.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Foods

rules.sourceTitle: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Animal Products
rules.notes: Specific limits for pesticide residues in animal products must be verified in the full regulation.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Animal Products

rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: If there is a risk of allergen cross-contamination, a precautionary allergen labeling statement is suggested, such as: “The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○” or synonymous terms.
rules.evidenceQuote: If there is a risk of a food product being affected by allergen cross-contamination, such as using the same plant, equipment or production line for food containing allergenic ingredients or additives, the following statement of precautionary allergen labeling is suggested: “ The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○” or the synonymous terms.

rules.sourceTitle: Revised Regulations on Nutrition Claim for Prepackaged Food Products
rules.notes: Specific requirements for nutrition claims on prepackaged food products must be verified.
rules.evidenceQuote: Revised Regulations on Nutrition Claim for Prepackaged Food Products

rules.evidenceQuote: Approved Food and Color Additives and Petitions Under Review

rules.limit: Prohibited from use in food, as listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30)
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: These substances are labeled as 'DELISTED'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) (labeled as "DELISTED").

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74) or evaluated by FEMA/JECFA for flavoring substances
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: The Substances Added to Food inventory provides a partial list; full compliance requires checking the referenced CFR parts.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively), and flavoring substances evaluated by FEMA* and JECFA*.

rules.limit: Verification required against FDA GRAS Notice Inventory and regulations
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additive Listings | FDA
rules.notes: Substances generally recognized as safe (GRAS) are exempt from the food additive definition and thus do not require premarket approval. However, their GRAS status must be substantiated.
rules.evidenceQuote: Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS)

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184)
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: The Substances Added to Food inventory provides a partial list; full compliance requires checking the referenced CFR parts.
rules.evidenceQuote: Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184).

rules.limit: Must be approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, as per 21 CFR Part 181
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: These substances are approved for specific uses under 21 CFR Part 181.
rules.evidenceQuote: Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.limit: Prohibited from use in food, as listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189)
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: These substances are labeled as 'PROHIBITED' or 'PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food (labeled as "PROHIBITED" or "PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS").

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: This is a guidance level.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: This is an action level. Specific action levels also exist for Brazil nuts, peanuts and peanut products, and pistachio nuts.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400

rules.parameter: Poisonous or deleterious substances (e.g., toxic elements, mycotoxins, persistent pesticides, radionuclides, other chemical contaminants)
rules.limit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool and referenced guidance documents/regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509)
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: The tool provides a consolidated list of tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Users must refer to linked guidance documents for detailed application.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Contaminant levels listed in the transparency tool include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.limit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling and established compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the claim.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable 'cross-contact' only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes and taken every precaution. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact... FDA guidance and regulations for the food industry states that advisory statements should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices and must be truthful and not misleading.

rules.foodCategory: Human food (establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold)
rules.parameter: Allergen preventive controls and accurate labeling
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Facilities must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).
rules.evidenceQuote: The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.parameter: Declaration of cochineal extract and carmine
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required as they are identified as allergenic substances.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement. Specific types of tree nuts, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish species must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways. The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... — OR — Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.

rules.parameter: Declaration of sulfiting agents (e.g., sodium bisulfite)
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required due to adverse reactions (e.g., asthma) in sensitive individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.foodCategory: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.
